epigraph.quote: Not sworn with the tongue. With the marrow.

blocks.text: No Apprentice. No student. No circle on the floor. The chalk has been erased. The candle sits unlit at the edge of the stage.
BROTHER JACK stands alone. Centre stage. Looking out at the empty seats.
He has done this before. At the end of every Phase One. He stands in the room after everyone has left. And he speaks.
to the empty house — and to you
That is not a small thing.
Most people leave before this moment.
Not dramatically. Not with an announcement.
The mirror shows something they were not prepared to see.
You did not find a reason.
Or you found one and came anyway.
He walks to the edge of the stage. Sits. Looks at the empty seats the way you look at something you have seen many times and still find worth looking at.
What is the story you carry.
When did you decide to carry it.
I am not going to ask whether you answered them.
That is between you and the work.
What I will say is this.
The answers do not have to be complete.
They do not have to be comfortable.
They do not have to be the answers you hoped to find.
And honest answers to those four questions —
held without performance, without management, without the protective layer of the version of yourself you show to rooms full of people —
Not the training. Not the technique.
The one who was there before any of this began.
And who will be there when all of it is done.
He stands. Walks back to the centre of the stage. Looks at where the chalk circle was.
Then he looks out at the empty seats again.
Phase Two is not going to ask who you are.
Phase Two is going to demand what you are made of.
It will ask you to go to the marrow.
Not the managed emotion that you have learned to produce on demand.
The place so far inside you that most people spend their entire lives never visiting it.
And it will ask you to bring what you find there into the work.
In service of a story that needs it.
For an audience that cannot name what it needs
but will recognise it the moment it arrives.
That is a significant thing to ask of a person.
A silence. The empty house. The unlit candle.
The particular nod of someone who has just made an agreement with the person in front of them.
That the student who is still here
has already answered the most important question Phase One asked.
With the fact of their continued presence.
very quietly — the close of Phase One
Then sets it down at the centre of the stage.

Not everyone does. Not because the lessons are too difficult. Because the questions are. And the people who leave — they leave honestly. The questions found something they were not yet ready to face. That is not failure. That is information.
But you stayed. Which means the questions found something that could bear the weight of them. Something that did not collapse when the mirror showed what was actually there.
That is the marrow. And Phase Two is going to need it.

Phase Two is the instrument. The body. The voice. The emotional architecture that makes carrying truth possible. It will go further inside than Phase One did. It will ask more. It will also give more — in the specific way that difficult things give: not comfort, but capacity.
Rest if you need to. Then return.

requirement.prompt: Post about what staying costs. Not what the curriculum gave you — what continuing required you to give. The thing you almost let stop you. And what moved you past it. Include #JoelhoodP1L5.
